q: What is the difference between a `StatelessWidget` and a `StatefulWidget`, and when do you choose each?
a: `StatelessWidget` holds no mutable state; its `build()` is a pure function of its constructor arguments and the `BuildContext`. `StatefulWidget` pairs with a `State` object that persists across rebuilds and can call `setState()` to schedule a new frame. Choose `StatelessWidget` for any UI element whose output is fully determined by its inputs (an icon, a label, a card). Reach for `StatefulWidget` when user interaction, animations, or async results need to mutate local data over time. Prefer keeping state as high or as low as it needs to be — unnecessary `State` objects add lifecycle overhead.
fj: StatefulWidget is the safe default; I'll use it everywhere.
fs: I default to stateless and only introduce `StatefulWidget` when the widget itself owns mutable state — otherwise I push state up or into a provider.

followups.q: Can a `StatelessWidget` ever rebuild?
followups.a: Yes — whenever its parent rebuilds and passes new constructor arguments, Flutter calls `build()` again on the stateless widget. It has no state of its own to preserve, so the widget object is simply discarded and recreated. The element persists and reconciles the new widget against the old one, which is why rebuilds are cheap even for stateless widgets.

followups.q: What is the role of the `State` object's lifecycle methods?
followups.a: `initState()` runs once when the element is first inserted; it is the right place for one-time setup like subscribing to streams or initializing controllers. `didUpdateWidget()` fires when the parent passes a new widget configuration so you can react to prop changes. `dispose()` is the teardown hook — cancel timers, close streams, and call `super.dispose()` here to avoid memory leaks and the 'setState after dispose' crash.

q: Explain Flutter's three trees: Widget, Element, and RenderObject. Why are widgets immutable?
a: Flutter maintains three parallel trees. The **Widget tree** is the developer-authored, immutable description of UI — cheap to create and discard every frame. The **Element tree** is the persistent, mutable counterpart: each element is instantiated once, holds a reference to its widget and its `RenderObject`, and acts as the `BuildContext`. The **RenderObject tree** does the actual work of layout and painting. Widgets are immutable because their role is only configuration; Flutter's reconciler compares old and new widget objects cheaply to decide whether to update an element's properties, avoiding full RenderObject reconstruction. This design lets `build()` return brand-new widget trees on every frame without performance cost.
fj: Widgets are the real UI objects that get rendered on screen.
fs: Widgets are config descriptors — cheap, immutable, thrown away each frame. Elements are the persistent identity layer. RenderObjects are what actually lays out and paints pixels.

followups.q: What is `BuildContext` really?
followups.a: `BuildContext` is just a handle to the widget's `Element` in the element tree. When you call `Theme.of(context)` or `Navigator.of(context)`, Flutter walks up the element tree to find the nearest ancestor that provides that type. Because it is a tree-position handle, passing a context captured in one widget into a completely different part of the tree will find the wrong ancestors — a common source of bugs.

followups.q: Why doesn't every widget produce a RenderObject?
followups.a: Only subclasses of `RenderObjectWidget` (like `Padding`, `SizedBox`, `ColoredBox`) create a `RenderObject`. Composition widgets like `StatelessWidget` and `StatefulWidget` only produce `ComponentElement`s that delegate to their `build()` output. This separation keeps the RenderObject tree shallow and fast while allowing arbitrarily deep widget composition.

q: How does `setState()` trigger a rebuild, and how do you minimize the scope of that rebuild?
a: Calling `setState()` marks the `State` object's element as dirty and schedules a frame. On the next frame Flutter calls `build()` on that element, diffing the returned widget subtree against the existing one and updating only changed `RenderObject` properties. To minimize rebuild scope: extract the part of the UI that actually changes into its own `StatefulWidget` so the dirty mark is scoped to a small subtree; use `const` for child widgets that never change so Flutter short-circuits their rebuild entirely; or push state into a fine-grained `ValueNotifier` / provider so only the listening widget rebuilds.
fj: setState re-renders the whole screen.
fs: I scope `setState` by extracting the changing subtree into its own widget — then only that widget's `build()` runs, not the whole parent tree.

code.label: narrow rebuild scope
lines: // BAD — whole Page rebuilds on every tap

followups.q: When is it safe to call `setState()` during the build phase?
followups.a: Never — calling `setState()` during `build()` throws a `FlutterError` because you would be marking the element dirty while Flutter is already in the layout/build pass. If you need to trigger a rebuild in response to a computation in `build()`, schedule it with `WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback()` so it fires after the current frame completes.

q: What is `BuildContext` and what is the 'context across async gap' bug?
a: `BuildContext` is a handle to the element's position in the tree. After an `await`, the widget may have been unmounted — its element removed from the tree — making the captured context stale. Using a stale context to call `Navigator.of(context)` or `ScaffoldMessenger.of(context)` throws because the ancestor lookup traverses a detached subtree. The fix since Flutter 3.7 is to check `context.mounted` after every `await` before using context; alternatively, capture the navigator/messenger reference before the await.
fj: I just use context after await like normal; it usually works.
fs: After every `await` I gate all context usage behind `if (context.mounted)` — the analyzer's `use_build_context_synchronously` lint enforces this.

followups.q: What is the difference between `State.mounted` and `BuildContext.mounted`?
followups.a: `State.mounted` has existed since early Flutter and is only available inside a `StatefulWidget`'s `State` object. `BuildContext.mounted` was introduced in Flutter 3.7 and is available on any `BuildContext`, including inside `StatelessWidget` or a callback that only has access to the context. Prefer `context.mounted` in new code because it works universally and is what the `use_build_context_synchronously` lint checks.

followups.q: When would you capture a reference before the await instead of checking `mounted`?
followups.a: When you need the object itself — e.g., `final nav = Navigator.of(context)` before the await — you can call `nav.pop()` even after the widget unmounts as long as the Navigator itself is still alive (it lives higher in the tree). This pattern is useful when you want navigation to happen unconditionally after an async operation regardless of the triggering widget's lifecycle.

q: What are Flutter Keys, when do you need them, and what bug does a missing key cause in a reorderable list?
a: A `Key` is an identifier Flutter uses to match a widget in the new tree to its corresponding element in the old tree. Without keys, Flutter matches by widget type and position. In a list where items can be reordered or removed, a position-only match causes Flutter to reuse the wrong state — a classic bug where swapping two `StatefulWidget` items results in each item keeping the state of its old position. Adding `ValueKey(item.id)` to each list item tells Flutter to preserve state by identity, not index. Use `ValueKey` for value-equal identifiers, `ObjectKey` for object-identity matching, and `GlobalKey` sparingly for accessing a widget's state from outside its subtree.
fj: I've never needed keys; the default behavior works fine.
fs: Keys are the reconciler's hint for identity. I reach for `ValueKey` whenever list items can be reordered so state follows the data, not the slot.

code.label: missing key bug
lines: // BUG — no keys, state stays in slot

code.label: ValueKey fix
lines: // FIX — ValueKey ties state to the item

followups.q: What are the risks of overusing `GlobalKey`?
followups.a: `GlobalKey` forces the element to be retained even if it moves to a completely different location in the tree, which prevents the garbage collector from releasing the associated `State` and `RenderObject`. Each `GlobalKey` lookup is an O(1) hash map operation but the memory and lifecycle cost accumulates. Legitimate uses are `FormState` access for validation and `RepaintBoundary` capture. For everything else, prefer `ValueKey`, `ObjectKey`, or a provider.

followups.q: What is a `UniqueKey` and when is it useful?
followups.a: `UniqueKey` generates a new unique identity each time it is constructed, guaranteeing Flutter discards the old element and creates a fresh one. This is useful when you deliberately want to reset a widget's state — e.g., forcing an `AnimatedSwitcher` to treat an otherwise identical child as a new widget, or resetting a `Form`. Be careful: creating a `UniqueKey` inside `build()` means every rebuild tears down and recreates the element, losing all state.

q: What do `const` constructors do for Flutter performance, and what is a 'rebuild storm'?
a: A `const` constructor instructs the Dart compiler to canonicalize the widget instance at compile time — every usage of an identical `const` expression returns the same object. Flutter's reconciler compares old and new widget references using `identical()` before diffing; if the reference is the same object, it skips the subtree entirely. A rebuild storm is when a `setState` high in the tree causes hundreds of descendant widgets to rebuild because they were constructed without `const`, even though their visual output would be identical. Marking immutable leaf widgets `const` (icons, labels, static containers) is one of the cheapest wins in Flutter profiling.
fj: const is just a style preference for immutable objects.
fs: `const` widgets survive a parent rebuild with zero work — Flutter sees `identical(old, new) == true` and skips the subtree. I treat missing `const` warnings as performance bugs.

code.label: missing const — rebuild storm
lines: // BAD — new Icon instance every build()

followups.q: Does `const` affect runtime behavior beyond performance?
followups.a: Yes — `const` objects are deeply immutable and their equality is referential (`identical`). This means two `const Text('Hello')` expressions anywhere in the codebase are the exact same Dart object. It also means you cannot have a `const` widget with any non-const field (e.g., a closure or a non-const color), which is why the compiler enforces it as a type error rather than a lint warning.

q: Walk me through Flutter's build/layout/paint pipeline and the constraints model.
a: Flutter's frame pipeline has three phases. **Build**: Flutter calls `build()` on dirty elements, producing a new widget subtree that is reconciled against the element tree. **Layout**: The `RenderObject` tree is traversed depth-first — each parent passes `BoxConstraints` (min/max width and height) down to its children, children size themselves within those constraints and return their chosen size up, and the parent positions each child. This single pass is O(n). **Paint**: Each `RenderObject` calls `paint()`, recording drawing commands to a canvas that is composited into layers and sent to the GPU. The mantra is: *constraints go down, sizes go up, parent sets position*. This is why an unbounded constraint (like putting a `Column` inside a `Column` without `Expanded`) causes 'unconstrained layout' errors.
fj: Flutter just renders whatever size the widget asks for.
fs: Constraints go down, sizes go up, parent sets position — one depth-first pass, O(n) layout. I reason about unbounded-constraint errors by tracing what the parent is passing down.

followups.q: What causes 'RenderFlex overflowed by X pixels'?
followups.a: A `Row` or `Column` has children whose combined `mainAxis` sizes exceed the constraint passed by the parent. The RenderFlex tries to give each child its intrinsic size; if the sum overflows, it logs the error and clips. Fixes: wrap the overflowing child in `Expanded` or `Flexible` to make it share available space, use `Overflow.visible` intentionally, or constrain parent size.

followups.q: What is `RepaintBoundary` and when do you add one?
followups.a: `RepaintBoundary` creates a new compositing layer for its subtree so that when it repaints, Flutter does not repaint its siblings. Use it around animations, scrolling lists, or any widget that repaints at high frequency while the rest of the screen is static. The tradeoff is memory — each boundary allocates an offscreen texture — so add it surgically, guided by the Flutter DevTools 'Highlight Repaints' toggle.

q: Compare state management options in Flutter: `setState`, `InheritedWidget`, Provider, Riverpod, and Bloc.
a: `setState` is local widget state — synchronous, no dependencies, zero boilerplate, but does not cross widget boundaries. `InheritedWidget` propagates state down the tree via `of(context)` and is the foundation everything else is built on, but hand-rolling it is tedious. Provider wraps `InheritedWidget` with `ChangeNotifier` and a friendlier API; good for small-medium apps but lookups are runtime and depend on widget position. Riverpod 2.x moves providers outside the widget tree, is compile-safe, and is independently testable — the community default for new projects in 2025. Bloc enforces strict event→state separation; best for large teams who need an auditable, event-driven contract. Choose by team size and app complexity, not hype.
fj: I just use Provider for everything, it works fine.
fs: I default to Riverpod for new projects — compile-safe, no BuildContext dependency, testable from plain Dart. I reach for Bloc when the team needs a hard event-sourced contract.

code.label: Riverpod provider + consumer
lines: // Consume in widget — no BuildContext needed

followups.q: What is the core architectural difference between Provider and Riverpod?
followups.a: Provider relies on `InheritedWidget` and therefore on widget position in the tree — the provider must be an ancestor of every consumer, and a `ProviderNotFoundException` at runtime indicates a missing ancestor. Riverpod providers live in a global `ProviderContainer`, completely decoupled from the widget tree, so you can read state from plain Dart tests, background isolates, or other providers without any widget context.

followups.q: When would you pick Bloc over Riverpod in 2025?
followups.a: Bloc's value is the explicit event→state contract enforced by the type system and the `bloc_test` library. On a large team (10+ engineers) this means every state transition is a named, versioned event that can be tested in isolation with zero mocks. If you need to reconstruct the full history of state transitions for debugging or auditing, Bloc's stream of `Transition` objects is invaluable. Riverpod is faster to build with on a small team but lacks Bloc's structural guardrails.

q: How does Dart's async model work? Explain `Future`, `async`/`await`, the event loop, and the microtask queue.
a: Dart is single-threaded per isolate. The event loop drains two queues in priority order: the **microtask queue** (high priority, drained completely before touching the event queue) and the **event queue** (I/O callbacks, timers, user events). `Future` represents an eventual value; `async`/`await` is syntactic sugar that desugars to chained `.then()` callbacks scheduled as microtasks once the awaited operation completes. The implication is that `await Future.value(42)` resumes in the microtask queue, not the event queue, so it yields to neither I/O nor UI frames — you need `await Future.delayed(Duration.zero)` to genuinely yield to the event queue. All UI rendering runs from the event queue, so a tight microtask loop can starve the UI.
fj: async/await makes Dart multi-threaded.
fs: Dart is single-threaded per isolate. Microtasks have priority over events — a tight `.then()` chain can starve I/O and the UI render loop, so I know when to use `Future.delayed(Duration.zero)` to yield.

followups.q: What is the difference between `Future` and `Stream`?
followups.a: A `Future` represents a single eventual value (or error) — it fires once and is done. A `Stream` is an asynchronous sequence of values delivered over time. Use `Future` for a one-shot async operation like an HTTP response. Use `Stream` for real-time data: WebSocket messages, sensor readings, Firestore `snapshots()`. `StreamBuilder` in Flutter listens to a stream and rebuilds the widget on each new event.

followups.q: What happens if an unhandled exception is thrown inside a `Future`?
followups.a: An unhandled rejection in a `Future` is caught by the `Zone`'s unhandled-exception handler — in Flutter that means it is reported to `FlutterError.onError` and logged as an unhandled async error, but it does not crash the app the same way a synchronous exception would. To surface it: either `await` the future in a `try/catch`, attach a `.catchError()` handler, or run the app in a custom `Zone` with `runZonedGuarded`.

q: What is the classic `FutureBuilder` pitfall of rebuilding the `Future` inside `build()`, and how do you fix it?
a: If you construct the `Future` inside `build()` — e.g., `FutureBuilder(future: fetchData(), ...)` — a new `Future` is created on every rebuild (triggered by any ancestor `setState`, theme change, or keyboard appearance). Each new future object resets `FutureBuilder`'s `connectionState` back to `waiting`, causing a flicker and re-firing the async call. The fix is to store the `Future` in state — initialize it once in `initState()` (or `didUpdateWidget()` if parameters change) and pass the stored reference to `FutureBuilder`.
fj: I call the async function directly in the builder argument, it's clean.
fs: I initialize the future in `initState()` and hold the reference in state — never construct it inside `build()`, otherwise every parent rebuild re-fires the request.

code.label: future rebuilt each frame
lines: // BUG: new Future on every build()

followups.q: What are `ConnectionState` values and what should you show for each?
followups.a: `ConnectionState.none` means no future was provided (future is null). `ConnectionState.waiting` means the async operation is in flight — show a loading indicator. `ConnectionState.active` is only for streams — data is arriving. `ConnectionState.done` means the future completed; check `snapshot.hasError` first, then `snapshot.data`. Always handle the error case explicitly rather than ignoring `snapshot.error`.

q: What are Dart isolates, how do they differ from threads, and when do you use `compute()`?
a: Isolates are Dart's unit of true parallelism. Unlike OS threads, isolates share **no memory** — each has its own heap and event loop. Communication happens exclusively via message passing through `SendPort`/`ReceivePort`, which copies data. This eliminates entire classes of race conditions and the need for mutexes. The tradeoff is that passing large objects is expensive due to deep copying. `compute(fn, message)` is Flutter's convenience wrapper that spawns an isolate, runs `fn(message)` on it, and returns the result as a `Future` — ideal for JSON parsing, image processing, or any CPU-heavy task that would otherwise jank the UI thread. `Isolate.run()` (Dart 2.19+) offers the same one-shot pattern without the Flutter import.
fj: I'd just run everything async on the main thread.
fs: Isolates are share-nothing workers — no mutexes, no data races, but copying large objects is expensive. I use `compute()` for anything that visibly drops frames in the DevTools CPU profiler.

followups.q: What is the difference between `compute()` and `Isolate.run()`?
followups.a: `compute()` is a Flutter-specific helper from `package:flutter/foundation.dart` that uses `Isolate.spawn` internally and integrates with Flutter's debug tooling (timeline events, error reporting). `Isolate.run()` is the equivalent Dart SDK function added in Dart 2.19 — it's framework-agnostic and slightly lower ceremony. Both spawn a one-shot isolate, but for non-Flutter Dart code prefer `Isolate.run()`. For long-lived isolates that exchange many messages, use `Isolate.spawn()` with explicit `SendPort` channels.

followups.q: Can isolates share a `Platform` channel or Flutter plugin with the main isolate?
followups.a: No — Flutter's platform channels are bound to the main isolate's root isolate. Background isolates spawned via `compute()` or `Isolate.spawn()` cannot directly call platform channels. As of Flutter 3.7, `BackgroundIsolateBinaryMessenger.ensureInitialized(rootIsolateToken)` allows a background isolate to invoke platform channels, but you must pass the `RootIsolateToken` from the main isolate before spawning.

q: Explain Dart's sound null safety: `?`, `!`, `late`, and what 'sound' means.
a: Dart's null safety (GA in Dart 2.12, mandatory from Dart 3.0) divides every type into nullable (`int?`) and non-nullable (`int`). Non-nullable variables are guaranteed non-null at runtime — the type system proves it at compile time, which is what 'sound' means: no implicit null escapes. The `?` suffix marks a type nullable; the postfix `!` is a null assertion that asserts non-null at runtime and throws `Null check on null value` if wrong. `late` defers initialization of a non-nullable variable — the compiler trusts you to assign before first read; a missed assignment throws `LateInitializationError`. Avoid `!` in production code; prefer early null checks or the `??` operator.
fj: I just put `!` everywhere to make the compiler happy.
fs: Sound null safety means the compiler proves null can't escape — no runtime surprises. I use `?` to document nullable contracts, `late` for dependency injection fields, and treat `!` as a code smell requiring a comment.

followups.q: What is a `LateInitializationError` and how do you prevent it?
followups.a: A `LateInitializationError` is thrown when a `late` field is read before it has been assigned. It replaces what would have been a `NullPointerException` in unsound null code. Prevent it by assigning `late` fields in `initState()` or the constructor before any code reads them; for complex initialization, consider using `late final` with a factory closure initializer (`late final x = compute()`) so the assignment is guaranteed on first read.

followups.q: What is the difference between `T?` and `T??`?
followups.a: There is no `T??` in Dart — the double-question form is the `??` null-coalescing operator, not a type annotation. `T?` is a nullable type. The `??` operator returns the left operand if non-null, otherwise the right — e.g., `name ?? 'anonymous'`. A related operator is `??=` which assigns only if the variable is currently null.

q: What is the difference between hot reload and hot restart, and what changes require each?
a: **Hot reload** injects updated Dart source into the running Dart VM and re-executes the `build()` methods of all dirty widgets. It preserves app state — navigation stack, form inputs, `State` field values. It does **not** re-run `main()` or `initState()`. **Hot restart** discards all state, resets the VM, and re-runs the app from `main()`. Required when: static or global variable initializers change, `initState()` logic changes, enum definitions change, or native (Kotlin/Swift) code changes. Hot reload is why Flutter's iteration loop is so fast, but misunderstanding the boundary leads to confusion when a change is invisible after reload.
fj: I just use hot restart for everything to be safe.
fs: Hot reload preserves state and re-runs `build()` — `initState()` is not re-fired. I hot restart only when static state or initialization logic changes and the app behaves strangely after reload.

followups.q: Why might a hot reload appear to have no effect on a layout change?
followups.a: If the changed widget's `build()` is not in the dirty set — for example because the widget is wrapped in a `const` constructor or its parent's rebuild is gated — hot reload may not re-execute that specific `build()`. Force a reload of the whole tree with hot restart, or temporarily remove `const` to confirm the change is visible, then re-add it.

q: What is the difference between `ListView` and `ListView.builder`, and when does laziness matter?
a: `ListView` with a `children` list creates all child widgets eagerly at build time — every widget in the list is instantiated, laid out, and painted even if off-screen. `ListView.builder` is lazy: it receives an `itemBuilder` callback and only calls it for indices that are within or near the visible viewport. For short, bounded lists (< 20–30 items) the difference is negligible. For hundreds of items, `ListView.builder` is essential — it caps widget construction and RenderObject creation to roughly the visible count plus a configurable `cacheExtent` buffer. `ListView.separated` and `ListView.custom` (with `SliverChildBuilderDelegate`) offer further control over separators and fine-grained sliver behavior.
fj: I always use `ListView` and pass all items in children.
fs: I use `ListView.builder` by default for any list whose item count might grow — eager construction is invisible on 10 items but catastrophic on 1000.

code.label: builder vs eager list
lines: // BAD for long lists — builds all at once

followups.q: What is `cacheExtent` in a `ListView.builder`?
followups.a: `cacheExtent` is the number of logical pixels beyond the visible viewport that Flutter pre-builds items for. The default is 250 pixels. Increasing it reduces visible pop-in when scrolling fast at the cost of building more off-screen widgets. Decreasing it (or setting it to 0) defers construction until items enter the viewport, which is better for very heavy items.

q: Compare Navigator 1.0 (imperative) with Navigator 2.0 / `go_router` (declarative) routing.
a: Navigator 1.0 is imperative: you call `Navigator.push()` and `Navigator.pop()` to manipulate the route stack. It is simple and sufficient for modal-style apps with no deep linking. Navigator 2.0 introduced a declarative `Router` API where the navigation stack is a function of application state — the URL or route configuration drives the stack, making deep linking and web URL management first-class. The raw Navigator 2.0 API is verbose, so the Flutter team maintains `go_router` as the official high-level abstraction. `go_router` uses a URL-like path syntax, supports guards (`redirect`), nested routes, and `ShellRoute` for persistent navigation rails; `go(path)` replaces the stack, `push(path)` adds to it.
fj: I just use push and pop, deep linking is an edge case.
fs: For any app that needs web URLs or deep links I use `go_router` — declarative, URL-driven, and officially maintained. For simple modal flows Navigator 1.0 is still fine.

followups.q: How does `go_router` handle authentication guards?
followups.a: `go_router` supports a global `redirect` callback (and per-route `redirect`) that fires before every navigation. You check your auth state and return a redirect path if the user is unauthenticated, or `null` to allow the navigation. Combined with a `GoRouterRefreshStream` backed by your auth provider, the router automatically re-evaluates guards when auth state changes, rerouting the user without any manual navigation calls.

q: What are Dart mixins, how do they differ from abstract classes and interfaces, and what does the `on` clause do?
a: Mixins let you share method implementations across unrelated class hierarchies without inheritance. A `mixin` cannot have constructors and cannot extend another class. You apply a mixin with `with`: `class A extends B with M`. The `on` clause restricts which classes can use the mixin — `mixin M on B` means only classes that extend `B` can apply `M`, enabling super calls (`super.method()`) resolved against `B`'s interface. This differs from abstract classes (which define a blueprint with optional implementation, instantiable only through subclasses) and Dart interfaces (every class implicitly defines an interface; `implements` requires you to re-implement all members). Dart allows multiple `with` mixins applied left to right — later mixins shadow earlier ones.
fj: Mixins are just like abstract classes but you use `with`.
fs: I reach for a mixin when I want to share behavior across classes that don't share a base class — like adding serialization or logging without a common parent. The `on` clause is for when the mixin needs to call `super` on a specific base.

followups.q: What is a `mixin class` in Dart 3?
followups.a: Dart 3 introduced the `mixin class` modifier which allows a declaration to be used both as a `mixin` (`with`) and as a standalone base class (`extends`). Previously you had to choose — a `class` could not be used with `with`. `mixin class` has the same restrictions as a mixin (no generative constructors that would need to be passed through).

q: What common performance issues cause jank in Flutter, and how do you diagnose them?
a: The main jank sources are: (1) expensive `build()` methods called too broadly due to missing `const` or overly wide `setState` scope; (2) synchronous heavy work on the UI isolate (JSON parsing, regex, crypto) that should be offloaded to `compute()`; (3) missing `RepaintBoundary` causing large subtrees to repaint every frame; (4) creating new lambdas or widget instances every `build()` that defeat `const`-based caching; (5) unthrottled `StreamBuilder` receiving high-frequency events. Diagnose with Flutter DevTools: the **Performance** overlay shows frame build/render times, **Widget Rebuild Stats** highlights hot widgets, and **Highlight Repaints** shows which subtrees repaint on every frame.
fj: I'd just add a loading spinner so users don't notice the lag.
fs: I profile first — DevTools Performance view to find dropped frames, Widget Rebuild Stats to find hot builders, then I fix causes in order: `const` first, narrow setState second, offload CPU third.

followups.q: What is shader compilation jank and how is it mitigated?
followups.a: On first run, Flutter must compile Metal/Vulkan shaders for each visual effect it encounters — clip paths, blurs, gradients. This compilation can take 16–100ms, causing visible stutter on first animation. Mitigation: use `flutter run --cache-sksl` to capture shader warm-up traces, then embed them with `--bundle-sksl-path` so shaders are pre-compiled at startup. With Flutter's Impeller renderer (default on iOS, opt-in on Android) this problem is largely eliminated because Impeller pre-compiles a fixed set of shaders at build time.

q: What is the 'setState after dispose' error and how do you prevent it?
a: When an async callback (timer, HTTP response, stream event) fires after the widget has been removed from the tree, the associated `State` object is in a 'defunct' lifecycle. Calling `setState()` on it throws `setState() called after dispose()`. Prevention: check `mounted` before calling `setState()` in any async callback. Better: cancel subscriptions, timers, and animation controllers in `dispose()` so the callback never fires. The `mounted` check is a defensive layer, not a substitute for proper cleanup.
fj: I wrap setState in a try/catch to swallow the error.
fs: I cancel everything in `dispose()` — that's the real fix. The `mounted` guard is a last line of defense, not a substitute for proper lifecycle cleanup.

followups.q: What is the correct order of operations in `dispose()`?
followups.a: Cancel/close your own resources first (timers, `AnimationController.dispose()`, `StreamSubscription.cancel()`, `TextEditingController.dispose()`), then call `super.dispose()` last. Calling `super.dispose()` first sets `mounted` to false and may interfere with cleanup that accesses widget properties.

q: How does `InheritedWidget` work under the hood, and how is it the foundation of `Theme.of(context)`?
a: `InheritedWidget` is a special `ProxyWidget` that stores data and registers dependencies. When a descendant calls `context.dependOnInheritedWidgetOfExactType<T>()` (what `Theme.of(context)` calls), Flutter walks up the element tree to find the nearest ancestor of type `T`, stores the dependency, and adds the descendant element to a dependent set. When the `InheritedWidget` is replaced with a new instance whose `updateShouldNotify()` returns `true`, Flutter marks every registered dependent as dirty and schedules a rebuild. This is O(1) notification — the runtime does not scan the whole tree; it only invalidates the cached dependent set.
fj: Theme.of(context) is just a global variable lookup.
fs: `InheritedWidget` keeps a dependent set per ancestor — when data changes and `updateShouldNotify` returns true, only registered dependents are marked dirty. It is O(1) fan-out, not a broadcast.

followups.q: What is the difference between `dependOnInheritedWidgetOfExactType` and `getInheritedWidgetOfExactType`?
followups.a: `dependOnInheritedWidgetOfExactType` registers the calling element as a dependent — the widget will rebuild when the ancestor notifies. `getInheritedWidgetOfExactType` (formerly `inheritFromWidgetOfExactType`) does the lookup without registering a dependency — a one-time read with no rebuild subscription. Use the latter in `initState()` or when you want to read the value once without subscribing to future changes.

q: Explain Flutter's rendering pipeline in detail: build, layout, paint, composite, rasterize.
a: Each frame begins when `SchedulerBinding` triggers via `Window.onBeginFrame`. **Build phase**: Flutter calls `build()` on all dirty elements, reconciling the widget tree into the element tree. **Layout phase**: Starting from `RenderView`, each `RenderObject` receives constraints from its parent, sizes itself, and returns its size — single depth-first pass, O(n). **Paint phase**: `RenderObject.paint()` is called, recording drawing commands (not pixels) into a `PictureRecorder`; `RepaintBoundary` creates layer break points. **Composite**: The layer tree is assembled by `SceneBuilder`. **Rasterize**: The composed scene is passed to `dart:ui`'s `FlutterView.render()`, which hands it to the GPU via Skia (older) or Impeller (new). The key insight is that Flutter bypasses the OS widget toolkit entirely — it draws to a canvas provided by the embedding.
fj: Flutter calls the OS to render widgets like React Native does.
fs: Flutter owns the entire pipeline from widget to pixel — build, layout, paint, composite, rasterize — all in process. There is no OS widget bridge; that is both its performance advantage and why custom platform views are costly.

followups.q: What is Impeller and why was it introduced?
followups.a: Impeller is Flutter's new rendering engine that replaces Skia. It pre-compiles Metal (iOS) and Vulkan (Android) shaders at build time rather than lazily at runtime, eliminating shader-compilation jank. Impeller also moves more work to the GPU via a scene-graph IR, enabling more predictable frame budgets. It became the default on iOS in Flutter 3.10 and is opt-in on Android; Skia remains the fallback on web and desktop.

q: What is `GlobalKey` and what are its legitimate use cases versus its misuse patterns?
a: `GlobalKey` gives a widget a globally unique identity that persists even when it moves to a different parent. It provides access to the element's `State` (`(key.currentState as FormState).validate()`) and its `RenderObject` (`key.currentContext!.findRenderObject()`). Legitimate uses: `Form` + `FormState` for programmatic validation, `ScaffoldMessengerState`, `NavigatorState` outside the widget tree, and capturing a widget to a `ByteData` image via `RenderRepaintBoundary`. Misuse: using `GlobalKey` to share state between sibling widgets instead of lifting state up or using a provider, or creating `GlobalKey` inside `build()` (new key every build = full widget teardown/recreation every frame).
fj: I use GlobalKey whenever I need to call a method on a child widget.
fs: I use `GlobalKey` only for `FormState` validation and `RenderRepaintBoundary` capture — both require direct object access. For everything else I lift state up or use a provider, which is cheaper and easier to test.

q: How do you test Flutter widgets? Explain unit, widget, and integration tests.
a: Flutter has three test layers. **Unit tests** (`test` package) test plain Dart logic in isolation — no widgets, no platform. **Widget tests** (`flutter_test` package) use `WidgetTester` with `pumpWidget()` to inflate a widget tree in a fake environment, simulate interactions with `tap()`/`enterText()`, and assert the widget tree with `find.text()` / `expect()`. They run without a device and are fast. **Integration tests** (`integration_test` package) run on a real device or emulator; they test full app flows including platform channels, animations at real frame rates, and database I/O. The recommended ratio is many unit tests, a healthy suite of widget tests, and a few critical integration tests.
fj: I just manually tap the app on a simulator to test.
fs: I write widget tests first — they are 10x faster than integration tests, catch layout regressions, and run in CI. I write integration tests only for critical user journeys like checkout flows.

followups.q: What is the difference between `pump()` and `pumpAndSettle()`?
followups.a: `pump()` advances the clock by one frame (default 16ms) and calls the frame callbacks. Use it when you control exactly what should have happened after one rebuild. `pumpAndSettle()` repeatedly calls `pump()` until there are no more pending timers or animations — convenient for waiting out an animation or a `Future.delayed`, but it can hang if there is a never-ending animation like a `CircularProgressIndicator`. In that case, use `pump(duration)` with a specific duration.

q: What is `StreamBuilder` and what are its pitfalls versus `FutureBuilder`?
a: `StreamBuilder<T>` listens to a `Stream<T>` and rebuilds its subtree on each emission, returning an `AsyncSnapshot<T>` with the latest event. Pitfalls: (1) like `FutureBuilder`, creating the stream inside `build()` reconnects and re-subscribes every rebuild — store the stream reference in state; (2) a high-frequency stream (e.g., sensor data at 100Hz) triggers a rebuild for every event, causing jank — throttle with `.throttleTime()` from `rxdart` or debounce; (3) forgetting to handle `ConnectionState.waiting` results in a null-data flash; (4) streams from providers do not close when the widget unmounts — prefer `StreamBuilder` over manual subscription management because it cancels the subscription in its `dispose()` automatically.
fj: StreamBuilder and FutureBuilder are basically the same.
fs: My first check with any `StreamBuilder` is: is the stream stored in state or recreated in `build()`? Second: does the stream frequency require throttling? I've seen 100Hz sensor streams reduce frame rate to 10fps.

q: How do `AnimationController` and `Tween` work, and what resource leak must you avoid?
a: `AnimationController` drives an animation value from 0.0 to 1.0 over a given `Duration` using the `vsync` mechanism (provided by a `TickerProviderStateMixin`) to sync with the display's refresh rate. A `Tween<T>` maps the 0–1 range to a meaningful range (e.g., `Tween<double>(begin: 0, end: 300)`), producing an `Animation<T>` via `.animate(controller)`. The critical resource leak: `AnimationController` holds a `Ticker` that fires every frame — if you forget to call `controller.dispose()` in `dispose()`, the ticker keeps running after the widget is gone, leaking memory and CPU forever. Always call `controller.dispose()` in `dispose()`.
fj: I create AnimationController in build() so it gets a fresh one each frame.
fs: I mixin `SingleTickerProviderStateMixin`, create the controller in `initState()`, and always dispose it in `dispose()`. Creating it in `build()` is a leak-per-frame.

q: What is platform channel communication in Flutter and when do you use `MethodChannel` vs `EventChannel`?
a: Platform channels let Flutter Dart code communicate with native (Kotlin/Java/Swift/ObjC) code via asynchronous message passing over a named channel. **`MethodChannel`** is request/response: Dart calls `invokeMethod('getName')`, native code handles it in `setMethodCallHandler` and returns a result. Use it for one-shot operations (biometrics, battery level). **`EventChannel`** is a stream: native code can push events continuously to Dart (sensor data, geofence events, audio levels). **`BasicMessageChannel`** is raw bidirectional messaging with a codec. All channels serialize via a `MethodCodec` (standard or JSON). Platform channels run on the platform thread, so heavy native work should be dispatched to a background thread on the native side to avoid blocking the main thread.
fj: I call native code with a global function from the dart:ffi package.
fs: I use `MethodChannel` for one-shot calls and `EventChannel` for continuous native streams. I always annotate the channel name as a constant shared between Dart and native to avoid typo mismatches.

followups.q: What is Flutter's FFI and when do you prefer it over platform channels?
followups.a: `dart:ffi` lets Dart call C functions directly in shared libraries (.so / .dylib) without going through the platform thread or a serialization codec. It is significantly faster for high-frequency calls (audio processing, image filters) because it avoids the serialization overhead and async hop. The tradeoff is complexity: you manage memory manually with `Pointer` types and must ensure thread safety. Platform channels are safer and easier for simple one-shot operations.

q: How does Riverpod differ architecturally from Provider, and what are `ref.watch`, `ref.read`, and `ref.listen`?
a: Provider wraps `InheritedWidget` — providers must sit above consumers in the widget tree and look up is runtime-typed, throwing `ProviderNotFoundException` if the ancestor is missing. Riverpod providers live outside the widget tree in a `ProviderContainer`, resolved at compile time. In a `ConsumerWidget`, `ref.watch(provider)` subscribes the widget to the provider and rebuilds it when the value changes — use in `build()`. `ref.read(provider)` reads the current value once without subscribing — use in callbacks and `initState()`. `ref.listen(provider, callback)` fires a side-effect callback on changes without rebuilding — use for showing snackbars or navigating on state transitions. Misusing `ref.watch` in a button callback causes a runtime assertion.
fj: I use ref.read in build() since it avoids unnecessary rebuilds.
fs: `ref.watch` in `build()`, `ref.read` in callbacks — mixing them up either misses reactivity or over-subscribes. I use `ref.listen` for side effects like navigation so they don't couple to the build cycle.

q: What is the Dart type system's approach to generics, and how does `covariant` work?
a: Dart generics are reified (available at runtime unlike Java's type erasure) and are by default invariant — `List<Cat>` is not a `List<Animal>` even if `Cat extends Animal`. This prevents unsafe writes. The `covariant` keyword on a parameter overrides this check for a specific method, asserting to the compiler that you (the developer) are taking responsibility for runtime type safety. It is most commonly used in `operator ==` or abstract interface methods where you know the runtime type will always match. Return types in Dart are covariant by default (a method returning `Cat` can satisfy `Animal` return type), but parameter types are not, which is the Liskov principle.
fj: I just cast with `as` when Dart complains about generics.
fs: Dart generics are invariant and reified — I reason about covariance explicitly with the `covariant` keyword only when I own the class hierarchy and can guarantee the runtime constraint.

q: How does Flutter's `ChangeNotifier` pattern work, and what are its performance limits?
a: `ChangeNotifier` is a simple observable class: subclasses call `notifyListeners()` to broadcast a change to all registered `addListener()` callbacks. Provider's `ChangeNotifierProvider` integrates this with `InheritedWidget` so all `Consumer<T>` or `context.watch<T>()` calls register as listeners. The performance limit is that `notifyListeners()` notifies **all** listeners regardless of which field changed — a large model with 10 listeners rebuilds all 10 even if only one field was relevant. For fine-grained reactivity, split into multiple small `ChangeNotifier`s or switch to Riverpod's per-provider granularity. Also: `ChangeNotifier` is not thread-safe — calling `notifyListeners()` from a background isolate will throw.
fj: I put all app state in one giant ChangeNotifier.
fs: One `ChangeNotifier` per domain concern — otherwise any field change blasts all listeners. For sub-field reactivity I use `ValueNotifier<T>` which is a typed single-value notifier.

q: What is `Slivers` in Flutter and when do you use `CustomScrollView` with sliver widgets?
a: Slivers are the building blocks of scrollable areas in Flutter — each sliver is a portion of the scrollable area that can have different layout and paint behavior. `CustomScrollView` lets you compose a scroll view from multiple slivers: `SliverAppBar` (collapsing toolbar), `SliverList` / `SliverGrid` (lazy item rendering), `SliverPersistentHeader`, and `SliverFillRemaining`. Use `CustomScrollView` when you need a single scroll physics for a screen that mixes a pinned header, a horizontal card row, a lazy vertical list, and a footer — effects impossible to achieve by nesting multiple `ListView`s. Under the hood, `ListView.builder` is a `CustomScrollView` with a single `SliverList`.
fj: I just nest a ListView inside a SingleChildScrollView.
fs: I reach for `CustomScrollView` when I need heterogeneous scroll segments — a collapsing app bar, a grid, and a pinned footer — in one unified scroll physics. Nested scrollables with the same axis are always a layout anti-pattern.

q: How do you handle dependency injection in Flutter, and what patterns are idiomatic?
a: Flutter does not have a DI container in its core, so teams use three patterns. **Service locator** via `get_it`: register singletons/factories at startup and retrieve them anywhere without `BuildContext` — great for services that don't need reactivity. **Provider/Riverpod**: providers are the DI mechanism for state and services inside the widget tree; constructor-inject dependencies into providers for testability. **Constructor injection with `late final`**: in `State.initState()`, read an inherited provider once and assign to a `late final` field, isolating the widget from the DI mechanism. For tests, swap out implementations by providing a mock at the `ProviderScope` or `get_it` level. Avoid singletons that hold mutable state without a reset mechanism in tests.
fj: I just use global variables for shared services.
fs: I use `get_it` for infrastructure services (analytics, auth client) and Riverpod providers for reactive state — each layer is independently mockable in widget tests.

followups.q: How do you override a Riverpod provider in tests?
followups.a: Wrap the widget under test in a `ProviderScope` with an `overrides` list: `ProviderScope(overrides: [myProvider.overrideWithValue(MockService())], child: MyWidget())`. This is the idiomatic Riverpod testing pattern — no global state mutation, no `setUp`/`tearDown` ceremony, and the override is scoped to exactly the test that needs it.

q: How do you manage app flavors and build configurations in Flutter (dev / staging / prod)?
a: Flutter itself has no built-in flavor system, so teams use two approaches. **Dart `--dart-define`** at build time: `flutter run --dart-define=FLAVOR=staging` injects constants readable via `const String.fromEnvironment('FLAVOR')`. Create a `AppConfig` class with static getters derived from this value. This is the Flutter-idiomatic approach and works without modifying native projects. **Native flavors**: create named product flavors (Android `productFlavors`) and schemes (iOS), each with a separate `google-services.json`, app bundle ID, and API key. Flutter reads the flavor name and branches configuration. The two approaches are often combined: native flavor handles bundle IDs and signing, `--dart-define` handles runtime API URLs.
fj: I just hardcode the staging URL and comment it out for prod.
fs: I use `--dart-define` for Dart-side config and native flavors for bundle IDs and signing certs. CI injects the dart-defines at build time, so no secrets ever live in source.

q: What are `Expanded` and `Flexible` in a `Row`/`Column`, and when does the difference matter?
a: Both `Expanded` and `Flexible` wrap a child inside a `Row` or `Column` and participate in flex layout. `Expanded` is `Flexible(fit: FlexFit.tight)` — the child is forced to fill its allotted flex space exactly. `Flexible` defaults to `FlexFit.loose` — the child is given the allotted space but may be smaller. The difference matters when a child has an intrinsic size smaller than its flex share: with `Expanded`, the child is stretched to fill; with `Flexible`, it keeps its preferred size and the remaining space is distributed to other flex children. Misusing `Expanded` on a widget that should shrink-wrap (e.g., a short text in a long row) causes unexpected stretching.
fj: Expanded and Flexible are the same thing.
fs: `Expanded` forces the child to fill — use it when you want proportional layout. `Flexible` lets the child be its natural size — use it when the child should not be stretched but should still participate in flex allocation.

q: What is `WidgetsBindingObserver` and how do you respond to app lifecycle events?
a: `WidgetsBindingObserver` is a mixin you add to a `State` class to receive app lifecycle notifications. Register with `WidgetsBinding.instance.addObserver(this)` in `initState()` and remove with `removeObserver(this)` in `dispose()`. Override `didChangeAppLifecycleState(AppLifecycleState state)` to react to: `resumed` (app in foreground), `inactive` (transitioning, e.g., phone call overlay on iOS), `paused` (app in background), and `detached` (engine running with no view, about to exit). Common uses: pause video in `paused`, save unsaved form data in `inactive`, refresh stale data on `resumed`.
fj: I don't know how to detect when the app goes to the background.
fs: I register a `WidgetsBindingObserver` to pause heavy resources on `paused` and resume them on `resumed` — always balanced with `removeObserver` in `dispose()` to prevent leaks.

q: How does `Bloc` pattern work in Flutter, and what is the difference between `Bloc` and `Cubit`?
a: `Bloc` (Business Logic Component) separates UI from business logic via a strict event→state pipeline: the widget dispatches a named `Event` object; the `Bloc` receives it in `on<Event>(handler)`, performs async work, and emits a new `State` object. `BlocBuilder` rebuilds when a new state is emitted. `Cubit` is a simplified Bloc without events — you call methods directly that emit states (`emit(LoadedState(data))`). Use `Cubit` when the state transition logic is simple and does not need the auditability of named events. Use full `Bloc` when you need an event log, `Transformer` for debouncing, or event deduplication. Both integrate with `flutter_bloc` package's `BlocProvider` and are independently unit-testable with `bloc_test`.
fj: Bloc is too complicated; I use setState and it works fine.
fs: I reach for Cubit for simple synchronous state and full Bloc when I need named events for audit trails or event transformers for debounce/throttle patterns. The test story (`bloc_test`) is excellent.

q: What is `RepaintBoundary`, how does it prevent repaints, and when is it counterproductive?
a: `RepaintBoundary` creates an independent compositing layer for its subtree. When a child inside the boundary repaints, Flutter only re-rasterizes that layer and composites it with the rest of the cached layers — the sibling widgets outside the boundary are not touched. This is a win when the bounded subtree repaints frequently (an animation, a live chart) while the rest is static. It is counterproductive when: (1) the boundary widget itself is large and the composited layer consumes more GPU memory than the savings; (2) the widget rarely repaints — the extra layer allocation just wastes memory with no benefit; (3) the parent also repaints every frame, so the boundary saves no work. Profile with 'Highlight Repaints' in DevTools before adding.
fj: I put RepaintBoundary around everything to be safe.
fs: I add `RepaintBoundary` surgically after profiling with 'Highlight Repaints' — it is a memory trade-off, not a free optimization. Wrapping a widget that repaints once per second with a RepaintBoundary wastes an offscreen texture for nothing.

followups.q: How do you capture a widget as an image using `RepaintBoundary`?
followups.a: Wrap the target widget with `RepaintBoundary` and assign a `GlobalKey`. Call `(key.currentContext!.findRenderObject() as RenderRepaintBoundary).toImage(pixelRatio: 3.0)` to get an `Image` object, then encode it with `.toByteData(format: ImageByteFormat.png)`. This is the standard approach for share-sheet screenshots and widget-to-image export.

q: How does Flutter handle deep linking and app links on iOS and Android?
a: Flutter supports two deep-link schemes: **URI schemes** (`myapp://path`) and **App Links / Universal Links** (HTTPS URLs verified by the OS). On Android, App Links require an `AssetLinksFile` at `/.well-known/assetlinks.json` on your domain and an `intent-filter` with `android:autoVerify="true"` in `AndroidManifest.xml`. On iOS, Universal Links require a valid `apple-app-site-association` file and Associated Domains entitlement. `go_router` handles both by registering a `routerDelegate` that parses the incoming URI and maps it to the right screen. Test with `flutter run --route=/detail/42` on desktop or `adb shell am start -W -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d 'https://example.com/detail/42'` on Android.
fj: I'd just use custom URI schemes; they're easier.
fs: I always recommend App Links / Universal Links over custom URI schemes in production — they cannot be hijacked by a malicious app, and the OS only routes the link if the domain-app association is verified.

followups.q: How does `go_router` process an incoming deep link?
followups.a: When the platform delivers a deep link, `RouterDelegate.setInitialRoutePath()` or `setNewRoutePath()` is called with the URI. `go_router`'s `GoRouterDelegate` parses the path, runs any `redirect` guards, and replaces the navigation stack with the matched route hierarchy. State restoration is handled by `GoRouterState`, which stores the last route in `RestorationBucket` so the app returns to the deep-linked screen after being killed and relaunched.

q: What is `AutomaticKeepAliveClientMixin` and when do you use it in a `TabBarView`?
a: By default, pages in a `TabBarView` or `PageView` are disposed when they scroll out of view — any state (scroll position, form input, loaded data) is lost when the user switches tabs. `AutomaticKeepAliveClientMixin` lets a `State` object opt in to being kept alive. You add the mixin, override `wantKeepAlive` to return `true`, and call `super.build(context)` at the start of `build()`. Flutter then prevents the element from being unmounted when offscreen. Use it for tabs that load data on `initState()` or maintain scroll position — but be mindful that keeping tabs alive increases memory usage, especially if each tab holds images or large lists.
fj: I reload data in every tab's initState so it always looks fresh.
fs: I use `AutomaticKeepAliveClientMixin` for data-loaded tabs where the reload latency would be noticeable — and I instrument memory in DevTools to verify I haven't kept 20 image-heavy tabs alive unnecessarily.

q: What is the difference between `MediaQuery` and `LayoutBuilder`, and when do you use each?
a: `MediaQuery.of(context)` returns device-level data: screen size, pixel density (`devicePixelRatio`), text scale factor, system insets (notch, keyboard height), and accessibility settings. It is available anywhere in the widget tree with a `MediaQuery` ancestor (automatically provided by `MaterialApp`). `LayoutBuilder` provides the constraints that the parent widget is passing to a specific widget — it is about layout context, not device context. Use `MediaQuery` for full-screen layout decisions (phone vs tablet breakpoints) or for reading the keyboard height to offset content. Use `LayoutBuilder` for responsive widgets that need to adapt to the space available in their specific slot in the layout, e.g., a card that renders differently at narrow vs wide widths.
fj: I use MediaQuery.of(context).size.width everywhere for layout decisions.
fs: I use `LayoutBuilder` for components that adapt to their slot — it correctly handles columns, cards, and sidebars that can be narrow even on a wide screen. `MediaQuery` is only for device-level signals like platform brightness or keyboard visibility.

followups.q: Why is calling `MediaQuery.of(context)` at the root of a widget a performance concern?
followups.a: Every widget that calls `MediaQuery.of(context)` registers a dependency on the `MediaQuery` `InheritedWidget`. When the keyboard appears, the system insets change, which triggers `MediaQuery` to update, which marks every dependent widget as dirty. A widget deep in the tree that only uses `MediaQuery.of(context).textScaleFactor` would also be rebuilt on keyboard appearance. In Flutter 3.10+, use `MediaQuery.textScaleFactorOf(context)` to depend on only that field.

q: How does Flutter's `Image` widget handle memory, and what should you do for large image lists?
a: Flutter decodes images to raw RGBA pixels in the `ui.Image` cache (`PaintingBinding.imageCache`), which by default holds the 100 most recently used images up to 100 MB. Large images (from the network or assets) are decoded at full resolution by default — a 4000×3000 photo decoded to RGBA takes ~46 MB. For lists: use `Image.network` with `cacheWidth`/`cacheHeight` to decode at display size, reducing memory by the square of the scale factor. Use `cached_network_image` package for persistent disk cache. For assets, set `ImageCache.maximumSize` and `maximumSizeBytes` to match your app's memory budget. Dispose `ImageProvider`s held in state to allow GC.
fj: I just use Image.network in a ListView; Flutter handles caching automatically.
fs: I always pass `cacheWidth` to `Image.network` in list items — downsampling at decode time is a 10× memory saving for thumbnails. I verify the image cache health in DevTools Memory.

code.label: cacheWidth for thumbnails
lines: // Decode at display size, not full res

q: What is Dart's `extension` feature and how is it used in Flutter codebases?
a: Dart extensions add methods, getters, and operators to existing types without subclassing. Declared with `extension Name on Type`, they are resolved statically at compile time — not dynamic dispatch. In Flutter, extensions are widely used to: add `.px` or `.sp` size helpers on `num`, add context-shortcut getters (`extension ContextX on BuildContext { ThemeData get theme => Theme.of(this); }`), add `.toColor()` to `String` for hex parsing, and add convenience builders on collections. They are purely syntactic — the generated code is identical to a top-level function call. They do not affect the runtime type and cannot override existing methods.
fj: I just write a utility class with static methods instead.
fs: I use extensions for context helpers — `context.theme`, `context.l10n` — so call sites read like property access rather than verbose `of(context)` chains. The zero-overhead, call-site-only resolution keeps the pattern clean.

q: Flutter uses Skia/Impeller to draw every pixel itself, while React Native delegates to native widgets. What are the performance and fidelity trade-offs of each rendering model?
a: Flutter owns the entire rendering pipeline: the framework builds a widget tree, performs layout, and rasterizes pixels directly onto a GPU canvas via Skia (legacy) or Impeller (current default on iOS, stable on Android as of Flutter 3.22). Because Flutter never touches native widgets, every pixel is identical across platforms and there is no bridge overhead at render time. The cost is that platform-specific widgets — a native iOS date picker, an Android chip — require `PlatformView`, which composites a native view into Flutter's layer tree at significant performance cost (GPU texture copy per frame). React Native takes the opposite stance: the JS layer drives actual native views (UIView, Android View), so the UI looks and behaves exactly like a native app and accessibility semantics are free. The bridge (legacy) or JSI (new architecture) serializes props and events between JS and the native thread; this crossing is cheap for static UIs but becomes a bottleneck when JS drives 60-fps animations directly. Flutter wins on pixel-perfect consistency and animation throughput; React Native wins on native widget fidelity and accessibility out of the box.
fj: Flutter is slower because it draws everything itself instead of using native widgets.
fs: Flutter owns pixels end-to-end — no bridge latency at render time, but PlatformViews are expensive. React Native delegates to native widgets — fidelity and accessibility are free, but JS↔native crossing caps animation throughput without the new architecture.

followups.q: What is Impeller and how does it fix the shader-compilation jank that Skia had?
followups.a: Impeller pre-compiles a bounded set of Metal (iOS) or Vulkan (Android) shaders at build time rather than lazily on first use. Skia compiled shaders on first render, causing 50–200ms stalls on first animation runs. Impeller eliminates this by moving all shader compilation to build time, trading a slightly larger app binary for stutter-free first frames. It became the iOS default in Flutter 3.10 and reached stable Android support in Flutter 3.27 (early 2025).

followups.q: How does React Native's new architecture (JSI + Fabric) change the performance comparison with Flutter?
followups.a: The new architecture replaces the asynchronous JSON bridge with JSI (JavaScript Interface), a C++ layer that lets JS hold direct references to native objects and call them synchronously without serialization. Fabric is the new concurrent renderer that supports React 18 features like `startTransition`. Together they close most of the latency gap that made high-fps gesture-driven animations difficult in React Native. However, Flutter's rendering is still fundamentally lower-overhead for complex custom painting because there is no JS runtime involved in the render path at all.

q: When would you recommend Flutter over React Native for a new cross-platform project in early 2026, and when would you go the other way?
a: Choose Flutter when: the product requires pixel-perfect, highly animated UI that must look identical on iOS, Android, web, and desktop; the team is comfortable learning Dart; custom UI components dominate over OS-native ones; or the app targets embedded/TV platforms Flutter supports. Choose React Native when: the team is already strong in TypeScript/React; the app must deeply integrate native platform components (ShareSheet, Maps SDK, NFC, CarPlay); you want to share business-logic code with a web React codebase via React Native Web or a monorepo; or the existing codebase is already in React Native. In practice the decision is often team expertise — both frameworks reached near-parity on core cross-platform features by 2025, and the marginal performance difference matters only for highly animated or graphics-intensive apps.
fj: Flutter is always better because Dart is faster than JavaScript.
fs: I start with team expertise and native-integration requirements. Flutter wins on custom UI throughput and platform parity. React Native wins on ecosystem overlap with web React teams and deep native API integration.

followups.q: Can a Flutter app and a React Native app coexist in the same monorepo?
followups.a: Yes, but they share very little tooling. Flutter uses its own CLI, Dart package manager (pub), and build system; React Native uses npm/yarn, Metro, and Gradle/Xcode. A monorepo (Turborepo or Nx) can host both, sharing only JSON data files, TypeScript type definitions (for a shared API contract), or OpenAPI-generated clients. Business logic cannot be shared between Dart and TypeScript without code generation or a shared C library via FFI/JSI respectively.

q: Explain the architectural difference between Flutter's widget-to-pixel rendering and React Native's shadow tree + native view synchronization.
a: In Flutter, the widget tree is a Dart object graph that drives an element tree, which drives a RenderObject tree. The RenderObject tree computes layout and emits draw calls to a `Canvas`, which Impeller rasterizes to the GPU with no OS widget involvement. In React Native, the JS thread maintains a virtual DOM (React tree), which is reconciled by the React renderer. The new Fabric renderer mirrors this to a C++ shadow tree that computes layout using Yoga (a cross-platform flexbox engine). The shadow tree then synchronizes its layout results to native views (UIView / android.view.View) on the main thread via JSI. The key difference: Flutter has one tree that owns the full pixel pipeline; React Native has three layers (JS VDOM, C++ shadow, native views) that must stay in sync, and any mismatch manifests as layout bugs or frame drops.
fj: Both frameworks work the same way under the hood.
fs: Flutter collapses widget→layout→pixel into one Dart-owned pipeline. React Native splits concerns across JS VDOM, C++ shadow tree (Yoga layout), and native views — more moving parts, but each layer is debuggable independently and the native views are real OS controls.

followups.q: What is Yoga and does Flutter use it?
followups.a: Yoga is Meta's open-source C++ implementation of the CSS Flexbox layout algorithm, used by React Native's shadow tree to compute view sizes and positions in a platform-agnostic way. Flutter does not use Yoga — it implements its own layout system with BoxConstraints and the constraints-go-down/sizes-go-up model. Flutter's layout is more general (it supports arbitrary RenderObject subclasses beyond flexbox) but requires learning Flutter-specific layout concepts rather than CSS-compatible ones.

q: Compare Flutter's hot reload with React Native's Fast Refresh. What does each preserve, and what breaks each?
a: Flutter's hot reload injects updated Dart bytecode into the running Dart VM and calls `reassemble()` on the root widget, which triggers a full `build()` sweep over the widget tree. It preserves `State` field values, the navigation stack, and any in-flight `Future`. It does not re-run `main()`, `initState()`, global variable initializers, or Dart `const` compile-time constants. React Native's Fast Refresh (successor to Hot Reloading) replaces changed modules using the Metro bundler's module hot-replacement API and preserves local React state for function components (via hooks) but discards class component state and side-effects. Both break when native code (Kotlin/Swift/ObjC) changes — a full rebuild is required. Both break when the initialization path changes (Flutter: `initState`; React Native: module-level `useEffect` with `[]`).
fj: Hot reload and Fast Refresh are basically the same thing.
fs: Flutter hot reload preserves `State` fields and re-runs `build()` but skips `initState()`. React Native Fast Refresh preserves hook state for function components. Both fail on native code changes. I document which setup steps require a cold restart so junior devs don't waste time reloading instead.

followups.q: Why might a Flutter hot reload not reflect a change to a `const` value?
followups.a: Dart `const` values are evaluated at compile time and baked into the binary. A hot reload injects new bytecode for changed functions but does not re-evaluate compile-time constants that were already inlined. To see a changed `const`, you need a hot restart (which re-runs `main()` and re-evaluates all statics) or a full rebuild.

q: Flutter uses Dart, React Native uses JavaScript/TypeScript. How do the language choices affect concurrency, type safety, and app size?
a: **Concurrency**: Dart uses isolates — share-nothing workers with message-passing — preventing data races entirely. JavaScript is single-threaded with Web Workers for parallelism; React Native exposes the same model but the JS runtime and the native bridge add coordination overhead. **Type safety**: Dart has sound null safety (Dart 3+) enforced at compile time — no null escapes the type system. TypeScript is structurally typed but unsound (type assertions, `any`, and `as` casts can introduce runtime errors); at runtime all TypeScript types are erased. **App size**: A minimal Flutter app bundles the Dart runtime, the Flutter engine (Skia/Impeller, ~4 MB compressed on iOS), and the compiled Dart AOT snapshot. A minimal React Native app bundles the JS engine (Hermes, ~1.5 MB), the React Native runtime (~3 MB), and the JS bundle. Both produce apps in the 8–20 MB range after tree-shaking, with Flutter typically ~2–4 MB larger for equivalent features due to the self-contained rendering engine.
fj: TypeScript is safer than Dart because TypeScript is more popular.
fs: Dart's sound null safety and isolate-based concurrency eliminate whole classes of runtime bugs. TypeScript's structural typing is flexible but unsound at runtime. The app-size difference (~2–4 MB) rarely matters on modern devices but is worth knowing for bandwidth-sensitive markets.

followups.q: What is Hermes and how does it affect React Native startup time?
followups.a: Hermes is Meta's JavaScript engine optimized for React Native. Unlike V8 or JavaScriptCore, Hermes pre-compiles JS to bytecode at build time rather than at startup, eliminating the JIT compilation phase. This reduces TTI (time to interactive) by 20–50% on cold start. Flutter achieves similar startup gains through AOT compilation of Dart to native machine code — both approaches move compilation work to build time.

q: A React Native team is evaluating a migration to Flutter. What are the key technical risks and migration strategies?
a: Key risks: (1) **Dart learning curve** — TypeScript developers are productive in Dart within days for basic syntax, but Dart's sound null safety, isolates, and extension methods require deliberate study. (2) **Native module parity** — any React Native `NativeModule` must be rewritten as a Flutter `MethodChannel` or replaced by a Dart package; audit `node_modules` for native dependencies first. (3) **State management paradigm shift** — React's component-local state and Context API do not map 1:1 to Flutter's `InheritedWidget`/Riverpod model. (4) **CI/CD reconfiguration** — Metro bundler, Fastlane scripts, and code signing profiles built for React Native must be rebuilt for Flutter's build system. Migration strategies: (a) strangler fig — new screens in Flutter within a host RN app via Add-to-App; (b) parallel builds — maintain both apps briefly while migrating; (c) full rewrite with a phased feature flag rollout.
fj: Just rewrite the whole app in Flutter over a weekend.
fs: I start with a native-module audit — every RN NativeModule that has no pub.dev equivalent is a risk item. Then I pilot Flutter via Add-to-App on one non-critical screen before committing to a full migration strategy.

followups.q: What is Flutter's Add-to-App and what are its limitations?
followups.a: Add-to-App lets you embed a Flutter engine inside an existing iOS or Android app. You instantiate a `FlutterEngine`, cache it in the application class, and present a `FlutterActivity` or `FlutterViewController` at specific navigation points. Limitations: the embedded Flutter engine costs ~10 MB of RAM and ~100ms startup on the first attach; multiple simultaneous engines multiply this cost. Only one `FlutterEngine` per process is recommended. Deep-linking between native and Flutter screens requires careful navigation event forwarding. Add-to-App is best for a phased migration, not a permanent architecture.

q: How do custom painting in Flutter (`CustomPainter`) and equivalent low-level drawing in React Native compare?
a: Flutter's `CustomPainter` gives you a `Canvas` API with direct access to Skia/Impeller drawing primitives: `drawPath`, `drawArc`, `drawImage`, `drawParagraph`, shader programs, and `saveLayer` for compositing effects. The painter runs in the Flutter render pipeline and is rasterized by Impeller on the UI isolate's raster thread — no overhead beyond the draw calls themselves. React Native has no built-in equivalent; custom drawing requires either: (a) react-native-svg for vector graphics (rendered as native SVG views), (b) react-native-skia (Meta/Shopify, embeds Skia as a native module), or (c) react-native-canvas which wraps a WebView Canvas. Each approach adds a native module dependency and extra serialization. For data visualization, charts, or game-like UIs, Flutter's `CustomPainter` is significantly simpler and faster because it is a first-class API with zero bridge overhead.
fj: React Native can do custom drawing with SVG, which is the same as Flutter's CustomPainter.
fs: Flutter's `CustomPainter` is first-class — zero bridge, direct GPU pipeline. React Native needs a native module (react-native-skia or react-native-svg) to achieve equivalent results, adding a dependency and JSI hop. For any canvas-heavy feature I default to Flutter.

q: Flutter and React Native both claim 'write once, run anywhere'. How does each handle platform-specific UI divergence — for example, a date picker or navigation patterns?
a: Flutter's `Material` and `Cupertino` widget libraries provide platform-styled widgets, but they are still Flutter-drawn pixels, not native widgets. `CupertinoDatePicker` looks like an iOS picker but is rendered by Flutter's engine, not UIKit. To use an actual native date picker, you need `showDatePicker()` (which uses Material Design) or a third-party package like `flutter_cupertino_date_picker`. For navigation, Flutter has a single `Navigator`/`go_router` stack across platforms. React Native renders the actual native `UIDatePicker` or `DatePickerAndroid`, so platform UI conventions are automatic. For navigation React Native Navigation (Wix) or Expo Router use native navigation primitives (`UINavigationController`, `FragmentManager`), giving native swipe-back gestures and transition animations for free. The trade-off: React Native gets platform fidelity for free on standard controls; Flutter requires explicit effort to match native platform conventions but gains consistency and customizability.
fj: Flutter apps look identical to native apps because it uses the platform's widgets.
fs: Flutter widgets are Flutter-rendered pixels — they look native by convention, not by implementation. React Native widgets are the actual OS controls, so accessibility, platform animations, and OS upgrades come for free. For consumer apps where iOS vs Android feel matters, this is a real architectural consideration.

followups.q: What is `PlatformWidget` pattern in Flutter and when do you use it?
followups.a: The `PlatformWidget` pattern (sometimes called an adaptive widget) is a Flutter idiom where you build a single widget that returns a Cupertino widget on iOS and a Material widget on Android, using `Platform.isIOS` or `Theme.of(context).platform`. Example: `PlatformSwitch` renders `CupertinoSwitch` on iOS and `Switch` on Android. This is the recommended approach when the app must respect platform conventions without using expensive `PlatformView`. Packages like `flutter_platform_widgets` provide ready-made adaptive widgets for the most common controls.

q: How do code-sharing strategies differ between Flutter and React Native when building for mobile, web, and desktop simultaneously?
a: Flutter supports all six platforms (iOS, Android, web, macOS, Windows, Linux) from a single Dart codebase with the same rendering engine. Business logic, state management, and most UI code is shared automatically; platform-specific code is isolated in `dart:io`/`dart:html` guards or `Platform.isXxx` branches. The web build uses either CanvasKit (Skia/Impeller in WebAssembly) or the HTML renderer — CanvasKit is pixel-perfect but downloads ~1.5 MB of WASM; the HTML renderer is lighter but has rendering differences. React Native targets iOS and Android natively; web sharing requires React Native Web (a separate library mapping RN components to DOM elements) and careful abstraction of navigation and platform APIs. Desktop on React Native is experimental via `react-native-macos` and `react-native-windows` (both Microsoft-maintained). In practice, React Native web+desktop code sharing requires more abstraction layers than Flutter, making Flutter the lower-friction choice for true six-platform coverage.
fj: React Native already runs on web so it's better than Flutter for multi-platform.
fs: Flutter's six-platform story is first-party and uses the same renderer everywhere. React Native web is a community mapping layer, not a native target — component parity gaps and performance differences are real and require ongoing maintenance.
